perturbation.original_text: 1.4. “Effective Date” means the date of execution of this Agreement by you .
perturbation.changed_text: 1.4. “Effective Date” means 30 days from the date of execution of this Agreement by you .
perturbation.explanation: Changing the 'Effective Date' to 30 days after execution creates an in-text contradiction. Since the agreement is effective 30 days after execution, but clause 3.2 states that Confidential Information may be exchanged by the Parties from the Effective Date, it becomes unclear whether information shared in those initial 30 days is protected, creating an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.2. Exchange of Confidential Information . Confidential Information may be exchanged by the Parties from the Effective Date until the Expiration Date, or, if the agreement(s) underpinning the Purpose have been entered into before the Expiration Date, then Confidential Info rmation may be exchanged until the obligations in such agreement(s) are still in effect.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the "Effective Date" to be 30 days after execution. Clause 3.2 states that Confidential Information may be exchanged from the Effective Date. Since the effective date is now 30 days *after* execution, it creates an ambiguity: is information shared *before* that 30-day mark covered? This is an in-text contradiction because the agreement implies information exchange can occur immediately, but protection only begins 30 days later. This aligns with the 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction' perturbation type as the 'Effective Date' is defined inconsistently, creating unclear obligations regarding the confidentiality of information exchanged in the initial 30 days.

perturbation.original_text: 2.2. Governing Law and Venue . All aspects of this Agreement, all matters arising from it, including disputes or lawsuits, and the relationship of the Parties relating to this Agreement, are governed by the laws of Luxembourg , and its courts shall have exclusive jurisdiction thereover . Nevertheless, each Party recognizes that a breach of confidentiality may cause irreparable harm to the other and that actual damages may be difficult to ascertain or inadequate in such cases. Each Party shall thus have the right to enforce this Agreemen t by injunction, specific performance, or other equitable relief, without a requirement to prove actual damages and without prejudice to any other rights and remedies that they may have otherwise.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.2. Governing Law and Venue . All aspects of this Agreement, all matters arising from it, including disputes or lawsuits, and the relationship of the Parties relating to this Agreement, are governed by the laws of Your local jurisdiction, and its courts shall have exclusive jurisdiction thereover . Nevertheless, each Party recognizes that a breach of confidentiality may cause irreparable harm to the other and that actual damages may be difficult to ascertain or inadequate in such cases. Each Party shall thus have the right to enforce this Agreemen t by injunction, specific performance, or other equitable relief, without a requirement to prove actual damages and without prejudice to any other rights and remedies that they may have otherwise.
perturbation.explanation: Switching the governing law to 'Your local jurisdiction' introduces ambiguity, as 'You' could be located anywhere, leading to uncertainty about which laws apply. This creates an in-text contradiction because the agreement is meant to be standard and the Teqball Holding, located in Luxembourg, would expect Luxembourg law to apply. It could potentially invalidate the agreement or cause disputes over jurisdiction and applicable laws. Especially in the case of disputes about which jurisdiction has power over a certain Confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.2. Governing Law and Venue . All aspects of this Agreement, all matters arising from it, including disputes or lawsuits, and the relationship of the Parties relating to this Agreement, are governed by the laws of Your local jurisdiction, and its courts shall have exclusive jurisdiction thereover . Nevertheless, each Party recognizes that a breach of confidentiality may cause irreparable harm to the other and that actual damages may be difficult to ascertain or inadequate in such cases. Each Party shall thus have the right to enforce this Agreemen t by injunction, specific performance, or other equitable relief, without a requirement to prove actual damages and without prejudice to any other rights and remedies that they may have otherwise.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the governing law and venue from 'Luxembourg' to 'Your local jurisdiction'. 'Your local jurisdiction' introduces ambiguity and thus an in-text contradiction, as 'You' could be located anywhere. This conflicts with the intention of a standard agreement where Teqball Holding, located in Luxembourg, would expect Luxembourg law to apply. This is a clear contradiction because the original agreement specifies Luxembourg law, while the changed agreement introduces uncertainty by referencing 'Your local jurisdiction,' which could be any jurisdiction, and thus contradicts itself. This adheres to the definition of Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3.4. Non-disclosure . Each Party shall hold the other Party ’s Confidential Information in strictest confidence and shall not disclose the other Party ’s Confidential Information to any third party , except to the Receiving Party’s own Authorized Persons on a need -to-know basis .
perturbation.changed_text: 3.4. Non-disclosure . Each Party shall hold the other Party ’s Confidential Information with reasonable care, and may disclose the other Party ’s Confidential Information to its employees and contractors without restriction .
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'strictest confidence' to 'reasonable care' and allowing disclosure to 'employees and contractors without restriction' weakens the confidentiality obligation. This introduces an in-text contradiction with the definition of 'Authorized Persons' in Section 1.1, which requires those with access to be bound by confidentiality terms 'at least as protective' as the Agreement. The modified clause allows disclosures without ensuring such protections, creating potential for breaches and disputes.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1.1. “Authorized Persons ” means a person or entity that a Party or a Party’s Group Company authorizes to access the Confidential Information for the Purpose, and who is bound by confidentiality terms at least as protective of the Confidential Information as this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the confidentiality obligation by changing 'strictest confidence' to 'reasonable care' and allowing disclosure to employees and contractors 'without restriction'. This directly contradicts the definition of 'Authorized Persons' in Section 1.1, which requires that anyone with access to Confidential Information be bound by confidentiality terms 'at least as protective' as the agreement. The modified clause allows disclosures without ensuring such protections, creating a clear conflict and inconsistency.

perturbation.original_text: 3.5. Information Security . Each Party shall secure the other Party’s Confidential Information and protect it from access by anyone other than its Authorized Persons, in the same way that it protects its own Confidential Information, but in all cases using a professional degree of care . The Receiv ing Party’s obligations relating to securing and protecting Confidential Information shall remain in effect for the later of either (i) 5 (five) years after the last date defined in clause 3.2 or (ii) until the Confidential Information may reasonably be considered as no longer confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.5. Information Security . Each Party shall secure the other Party’s Confidential Information with minimal security measures. The Receiving Party’s obligations relating to securing and protecting Confidential Information shall immediately cease upon the termination of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Weakening the security requirements to 'minimal security measures' and terminating the obligations upon agreement termination creates an in-text contradiction with the Purpose and the clause that defines how long the Parties should keep confidential information in safety, found on clause 3.5. Making the measures minimal makes the other clauses not binding to each other.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.5. Information Security . Each Party shall secure the other Party’s Confidential Information with minimal security measures. The Receiving Party’s obligations relating to securing and protecting Confidential Information shall immediately cease upon the termination of this agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the security requirements to 'minimal security measures' and terminates the obligations upon agreement termination. The contradicted text is the same clause but after the changes were made. By making the security measures minimal and terminating obligations upon agreement termination, there's a direct contradiction with the original intent of ensuring the confidentiality of information beyond the agreement's termination as the original clause defined a period for which confidential information must be secured. This falls under the 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction' definition because the key obligation (information security) is defined inconsistently within the document, creating uncertainty.

perturbation.original_text: 3.8. Return of Confidential Information . Immediately upon the Disclosing Party ’s request or by the last date defined in clause 3.2, the Receiving Party shall return /or destroy, at the Disclosing Party ’s discretion and expense, all the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information in its possession, irrespective of the media, and certify execution of this obligation in writing. This obligation shall not apply to (i) copies retained in compliance with a Party’s legal or regulatory obligations or (ii) copies made as part of preexisting data back -up proce sses, if such copies a re not accessible to the Receiving Party’s st aff or Authorized Persons in the normal course of business.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.8. Return of Confidential Information . The Receiving Party is not obligated to return or destroy any Confidential Information and may retain it indefinitely, irrespective of the Disclosing Party's request or the date defined in clause 3.2.
perturbation.explanation: Stating the Receiving Party is not obligated to return or destroy any Confidential Information contradicts the original intention of a mutual non-disclosure agreement. This creates an in-text contradiction since the agreement outlines strict measures of how the receiving party can treat the confidential information. This change allows the Receiving Party to retain and potentially use the information indefinitely, undermining the protections intended for the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.4. Non-disclosure . Each Party shall hold the other Party ’s Confidential Information with reasonable care, and may disclose the other Party ’s Confidential Information to its employees and contractors without restriction .
perturbation.justification: The changed text states that the Receiving Party is not obligated to return or destroy any Confidential Information and may retain it indefinitely. This directly contradicts section 3.4, which states that each party shall hold the other party's Confidential Information with reasonable care. The changed text removes the obligation to return or destroy the information, thus fundamentally conflicting with the requirement to handle it with reasonable care and implying a lack of restrictions on its use or disclosure, which contrasts the original agreement's intention as highlighted in the perturbation explanation.
